coredata.dc:title: In situ growth of nano-ZnO/GQDs on cellulose paper for dual repelling function against water and bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Meiling
Feng, Qiming
Liu, Hui
Wu, Yueru
Wang, Zhiwei
coredata.dc:description: To expand the application of cellulose paper for food and drug packaging, in this study, an impregnation method was used to allow Zn2+ to enter the fibers of a cellulose paper substrate. A hydrothermal synthesis method was used to enable the in situ growth of nano-ZnO and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the surface of the paper to improve the antibacterial efficiency of the cellulose paper. This functional paper was treated with stearic acid to improve its hydrophobicity. This resulted in the preparation of nano-ZnO/GQDs hydrophobic paper with an antibacterial efficiency of 85.7% and a contact angle of 122.8° with water.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the hydrothermal synthesis of GQDs-ZnO doped onto cellulose paper and multiple-barrier hydrophobic treatment.

Characterization of GQDs: (a) TEM and size distribution, (b) HR-TEM, (c) Raman diagram, (d) FT-IR spectrum, (e) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and photo, (f) Photoluminescence spectrum excited by 320–440 nm.
Characterization results of the functional paper: (a) Structure schematic diagram, (b) SEM morphology characteristics, (c) The XRD spectra, (d) Antibacterial efficiency and water contact angle.

head.ce:title: In situ growth of nano-ZnO/GQDs on cellulose paper for dual repelling function against water and bacteria

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Light Industrial and Food Engineering College of Guangxi University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To expand the application of cellulose paper for food and drug packaging, in this study, an impregnation method was used to allow Zn2+ to enter the fibers of a cellulose paper substrate. A hydrothermal synthesis method was used to enable the in situ growth of nano-ZnO and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) on the surface of the paper to improve the antibacterial efficiency of the cellulose paper. This functional paper was treated with stearic acid to improve its hydrophobicity. This resulted in the preparation of nano-ZnO/GQDs hydrophobic paper with an antibacterial efficiency of 85.7% and a contact angle of 122.8° with water.

ce:section.ce:para: Cellulose paper has been widely used as a packaging material for food and drugs due to its low cost, biodegradability, and renewability [1]. However, the food and drugs industry has specific antibacterial and hydrophobic requirements [2]. Therefore, in order to expand the application of cellulose paper for food and drug packaging, it is necessary to improve its antibacterial activity and hydrophobicity. Because of its nano-size effect and photocatalytic activity, nano-ZnO can be used as an antibacterial agent, as well as a building block to assist in the construction of micro-nano structures, while endowing cellulose paper with hydrophobicity [3,4]. Many methods are used to prepare nano-ZnO functional paper. For example, when Zn2+ is added to pulp fiber, a hydrothermal reaction allows nano-ZnO to be grown on the fiber [5]. Nano-ZnO functional paper is then obtained through a papermaking process. Alternatively, nano-ZnO can be coated directly onto the surface of the cellulose paper [6,7]. In a third method, the deposition of a ZnO seed layer on paper substrates can be performed by radio frequency sputtering, followed by the generation of nano-ZnO crystals through hydrothermal synthesis [8]. There have been no reports on the direct growth of nano-ZnO in situ on cellulose paper fibers. Accordingly, in this paper, Zn2+ was impregnated into the fibers of a paper substrate and then added into a sodium hydroxide solution to synthesize nano-ZnO functional paper by a one-step hydrothermal method.

A single ZnO molecule can only be excited by ultraviolet light, and cannot make use of most of the light provided by sunlight, including light in the visible range [9]. To expand the excitation wavelength range and to make effective use of visible light, in this study, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were added during the hydrothermal synthesis stage of preparing nano-ZnO functional paper to obtain nano-ZnO/GQDs functional paper. Because nano-ZnO/GQDs functional paper contains micro-nano structures, hydrophobic substances can be introduced onto it, thereby reducing its surface energy and adding hydrophobicity [10]. Here, stearic acid was used to hydrophobically modify nano-ZnO/GQDs functional paper by vapor condensation deposition, resulting in nano-ZnO/GQDs hydrophobic paper. By this method, nano-ZnO can be grown in situ on cellulose paper, strong binding with paper, sustainable antibacterial, GQDs was introduced to enhance the antibacterial efficiency under visible light, and stearic acid is deposited on the surface of functional paper by vapor condensation, which is easy to operate.

Fig. 1 shows the schematic illustration for the hydrothermal synthesis of GQDs-ZnO doped onto cellulose paper and multiple-barrier hydrophobic treatment. GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis with glucose as the primary raw material [11]. Nano-ZnO functional paper by a one-step hydrothermal method. GQDs were added during the hydrothermal synthesis stage of preparing nano-ZnO functional paper to obtain nano-ZnO/GQDs functional paper. Stearic acid was used to hydrophobically modify to obtain nano-ZnO/GQDs hydrophobic paper.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the TEM image and size distribution of GQDs. The average particle diameter of the prepared GQDs is 6.3 ± 1.5 nm; the wide distribution ranging from 3 nm to 10 nm indicates that the size of GQDs produced by this method is not uniform [12]. Fig. 2(b) shows the HR-TEM of GQDs, in which some GQDs have been marked with yellow circles and the crystal grid can be clearly observed. Fig. 2(c) shows the Raman spectrum of GQDs under the excitation of a wavelength of 325 nm. The two peaks seen, located at 1372 cm−1 and 1597 cm−1, are the D peak (blue) and the G peak (red), respectively. The D peak is due to defects present in the sample crystal, and the G peak is due to the sp2 carbon atom pair in the carbon ring or carbon chain [13]. The D and G peaks correspond to the disordered carbon structure and ordered graphite structure, respectively. The G peak is higher than the D peak, indicating that there are few GQDs defects. Fig. 2(d) shows the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs: the absorption peak at 1152 cm−1 corresponds to the CC vibration, the main composition structure of GQDs; the two weak absorption peaks at 1459 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 are absorption peaks from C-H bonds; 1130 cm−1 is the vibration peak of C-O; the broad absorption peak at 3300 cm−1 is the vibrational peak of O–H. Fig. 2(e) shows that the prepared GQDs have no obvious absorption peaks in the ultraviolet region. However, there is a shoulder peak at 260 nm, which may be due to the presence of functional groups (such as carboxyl groups) containing lone pair electrons [14]. This characteristic is in line with the optical characteristics of semiconductor GQDs. The prepared GQD aqueous solution was placed in a colorimetric dish; under natural light, the solution was pale yellow, and excited blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light with a wavelength of 365 nm. This is consistent with the literature [15]. Fig. 2(f) shows that GQD aqueous solution excited at wavelength of 320–410 nm, the intensity of the photoluminescence (PL) peak of the solution initially increased and then weakened as the excitation wavelength increased. When the excitation wavelength was 380 nm, the luminous intensity was at its maximum. Under the excitation wavelength of 410–440 nm, the intensity of the PL peak decreased with increasing excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization results of the functional paper
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 (a) shows the structural schematic diagram of nano-ZnO/GQDs hydrophobic paper. This diagram illustrates that GQDs and stearic acid may be attached to the nano-ZnO crystals, as well as the antibacterial properties and of the hydrophobicity prepared functional paper. Fig. 3(b) shows the SEM of the functional paper, including the control, ZnO (nano-ZnO functional paper), QZnO (GQDs/nano-ZnO functional paper), and SQZnO (stearic acid/GQDs/nano-ZnO functional paper). The fiber of the base paper is seen to be smooth. Nano-ZnO grows on fiber paper, the particles are uniform, small and complete, in the shape of grains of rice. After the addition of GQDs, the number of nano-ZnO crystals was slightly reduced, but the shape of the crystals was not affected. After the hydrophobic treatment by stearic acid, the stearic acid covered the nano-ZnO crystals, but the crystal shape remained unchanged, as can be seen from the XRD pattern in Fig. 3(c). Additionally, the peak before 30° in the XRD pattern is the characteristic peak of cellulose. There are sharp diffraction peaks after 30°, including three characteristic peaks of (100), (002), and (101), which determine the crystal structure to be a hexagonal wurtzite structure, based on comparison with the standard card JCPDS (Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards) card 36–1451 of ZnO [16]. After the addition of GQDs and hydrophobic treatment, the characteristic peaks did not change, indicating that the addition of GQDs and the hydrophobic treatment did not change the structure of the nano-ZnO crystals. Fig. 3(d) depicts the antibacterial efficiency and water contact angle of the functional paper. It can be seen here that the nano-ZnO functional paper has some antibacterial efficiency, but after adding 0.05 g·L-1 GQD solution, the antibacterial efficiency of the functional paper reached 83.3%, which is 8.97% higher than that of the nano-ZnO functional paper. This is because the quantum size effect of GQDs endows it with more oxygen-containing functional groups and causes it to increase the oxidative stress effect for killing bacteria [17]. After 10 min of hydrophobic treatment, the antibacterial efficiency of the functional paper was slightly improved, and the contact angle reached 122.8°. This may be because the deposition of stearic acid introduced the hydrophobic group methyl, so that it has the hydrophobic property [18]. After the addition of stearic acid, the stearic acid covered the nano-ZnO crystals, leading to a decline in antibacterial performance. However, due to the improvement of the hydrophobic performance, the bacterial adhesion ability was reduced, so the overall antibacterial efficiency showed a slight increase.

A paper-based packaging material with bacterial and water dual repelling functions was obtained. GQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using glucose and concentrated sulfuric acid. The average particle size was 6.3 ± 1.5 nm and blue fluorescence was excited in a wide wavelength range. Nano-ZnO and GQDs were grown on the surface of cellulose paper by hydrothermal synthesis, and stearic acid was loaded onto the surface by vapor deposit. After adding 0.05 g·L-1 GQD solution and hydrophobic treatment for 10 min, the paper-based packaging material with an antibacterial efficiency of 85.7% and a contact angle of 122.8° was obtained.

Meiling Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Qiming Feng: Investigation, Data curation, Resources. Hui Liu: Validation, Visualization. Yueru Wu: Formal analysis, Software. Zhiwei Wang: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21868004, 31660182), the Key Research and Development Plan of Guangxi Province (GuikeAB419259013), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (2017GXNSFAA198200).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.128838.

ce:section.ce:para:
